heroOverview: Myositis refers to a group of inflammatory muscle diseases in which the immune system attacks skeletal muscle fibres, causing progressive weakness, pain, and fatigue. The idiopathic inflammatory myopathies — the umbrella category that includes polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and inclusion body myositis — affect roughly 5 to 22 persons per 100,000 worldwide, with the incidence rising over recent decades partly because of improved recognition. Muscle inflammation that is not attributed to a specific autoimmune subtype is coded as myositis, unspecified (ICD-10: M60.9) and is managed by identifying the closest clinical pattern and treating accordingly. Most forms respond to immunosuppression, though the pace of recovery varies widely by subtype, age, and whether interstitial lung disease or malignancy is present.

keyStats.prevalence: Idiopathic inflammatory myopathies affect approximately 5–22 per 100,000 people; dermatomyositis and polymyositis together account for the majority of cases (Orphanet, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Dermatomyositis and polymyositis are roughly twice as common in women; inclusion body myositis predominantly affects men over 50
keyStats.avgAge: Dermatomyositis has a bimodal peak in childhood (5–15 years) and adults (45–65 years); polymyositis typically presents after age 20; inclusion body myositis after 50
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 50,000–75,000 people are living with an idiopathic inflammatory myopathy in the United States at any one time (NIH, 2022)

definition: Myositis, unspecified (ICD-10: M60.9) is a diagnosis of inflammatory muscle disease when a specific histological or serological subtype has not yet been established, or when the clinical picture fits the broad category without meeting full criteria for a named entity. The idiopathic inflammatory myopathies are autoimmune disorders in which autoreactive T lymphocytes, B cells, and innate immune effectors invade skeletal muscle, producing fibre necrosis, inflammation, and ultimately weakness. The main recognised subtypes are polymyositis, dermatomyositis, inclusion body myositis, immune-mediated necrotising myopathy, and anti-synthetase syndrome. Myositis can also arise secondary to infection (viral, bacterial, parasitic), drug toxicity (statins, checkpoint inhibitors), or as a paraneoplastic phenomenon in adults over 40. Because the unspecified code encompasses a heterogeneous group, careful clinical, serological, electrophysiological, and histopathological evaluation is needed to narrow the diagnosis and select the most effective treatment. Management falls primarily under rheumatology, with orthopaedics and neurology involvement for complicated cases. Without adequate treatment, progressive muscle weakness can impair ambulation, swallowing, and breathing.

typesClassification.description: Proximal limb and trunk weakness without skin involvement; predominantly affects adults over 20, more often women; responds to corticosteroids though the response is less reliable than in dermatomyositis.

Proximal muscle weakness with characteristic skin changes including heliotrope rash around the eyes and Gottron papules over the knuckles; occurs in both children and adults; associated with interstitial lung disease and, in adults, an increased malignancy risk.

The most common inflammatory myopathy in those over 50; causes slowly progressive distal and proximal weakness with characteristic finger flexor and quadriceps involvement; largely refractory to immunosuppression.
Severe proximal weakness with striking muscle enzyme elevation; associated with anti-SRP or anti-HMGCR antibodies; may be triggered by statins; requires aggressive immunosuppression.

A subset with myositis, interstitial lung disease, arthritis, mechanic's hands, and Raynaud phenomenon; driven by antibodies against aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases such as anti-Jo-1; often requires long-term combination therapy.

Muscle inflammation caused by viral (influenza, SARS-CoV-2, enteroviruses), bacterial (Staphylococcus aureus pyomyositis), or parasitic (Trichinella, toxoplasma) pathogens; managed by treating the underlying infection.

primarySymptoms: Progressive proximal muscle weakness — difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or lifting the arms above the head — is the defining feature and is typically symmetric.
Muscle pain and tenderness on palpation, particularly in the thighs, upper arms, and shoulder girdle, which may precede measurable weakness.
Profound fatigue that is disproportionate to the degree of exertion and does not resolve with rest.
Difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) when pharyngeal or oesophageal muscles are involved, occurring in up to 30% of polymyositis and dermatomyositis patients.
Skin rashes in dermatomyositis — heliotrope discolouration of the upper eyelids, Gottron papules over the metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints, and a shawl-sign rash across the shoulders.
Shortness of breath with exertion or at rest, reflecting respiratory muscle weakness or associated interstitial lung disease.
Hoarseness or a nasal quality to the voice when laryngeal and palatal muscles are affected.
Joint pain and morning stiffness, especially in the hands, wrists, and knees, in the anti-synthetase syndrome subset.
Thickened, cracked skin on the fingertips and lateral fingers ('mechanic's hands') in anti-synthetase syndrome.
Grip weakness and foot drop in inclusion body myositis, where distal muscles are affected in addition to proximal ones.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing it is harder than usual to get up from low seats or to climb stairs without using your hands.
Persistent muscle aching in the thighs or upper arms after light or no activity.
Unexplained creatine kinase elevation found on routine blood work.
New skin rash over the knuckles or around the eyes without obvious cause.
emergencySigns: Severe respiratory distress or rapidly worsening breathlessness suggesting respiratory muscle failure.
Inability to swallow saliva or liquids, raising the risk of aspiration pneumonia.
Profound generalised weakness developing over days rather than weeks (consider acute rhabdomyolysis or rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease).
Fever, very high creatine kinase, and muscle swelling pointing to bacterial pyomyositis or necrotising fasciitis.

causes.cause: Autoimmune T-cell and B-cell attack on muscle
causes.description: In polymyositis and dermatomyositis, autoreactive CD8+ cytotoxic T cells invade and destroy muscle fibres directly, while type I interferons and complement activation damage small vessels supplying muscle, triggering a self-sustaining inflammatory cycle that produces fibre necrosis and weakness.

Antibodies such as anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, anti-SRP, and anti-HMGCR activate downstream immune effectors. Anti-HMGCR antibodies, in particular, arise in immune-mediated necrotising myopathy and can be triggered by statin therapy in genetically susceptible individuals.

Influenza A and B, SARS-CoV-2, enteroviruses, and HIV can cause direct myocyte injury and trigger post-infectious immune-mediated muscle inflammation; viral myositis is typically self-limiting but can be severe, especially influenza-associated myositis in children.

Statins are the most common pharmacological cause; they inhibit the mevalonate pathway in muscle, impairing mitochondrial function and coenzyme Q10 synthesis. Immune checkpoint inhibitors used in cancer treatment trigger immune-mediated myositis in 0.4–1.5% of patients.

In adults over 40, dermatomyositis and polymyositis can reflect an immune response against a hidden tumour (commonly ovarian, lung, colorectal, or nasopharyngeal cancer), in which anti-tumour antibodies and T cells cross-react with muscle antigens.

causes.cause: Genetic susceptibility combined with environmental triggers
causes.description: HLA-DRB1*03:01 and other class II alleles are over-represented in myositis patients. A two-hit model proposes that a genetically primed immune system mounts a sustained response after exposure to a triggering infection, ultraviolet radiation, or drug.

riskFactors.description: Dermatomyositis and polymyositis are approximately twice as prevalent in women, reflecting the broader female predominance of autoimmune diseases; inclusion body myositis, by contrast, affects men more than women at a ratio of about 3:1.
Incidence of most inflammatory myopathies rises with age; inclusion body myositis almost exclusively affects those over 50, and the adult form of dermatomyositis peaks between 45 and 65.

Statins produce a spectrum of muscle toxicity from mild myalgia to immune-mediated necrotising myopathy; the necrotising form persists after statin withdrawal and requires immunosuppression, occurring in roughly 2 per 100,000 statin users annually.

Checkpoint inhibitors (PD-1, PD-L1, CTLA-4 blockers) unleash the immune system against cancer but can trigger inflammatory myopathy in 0.4–1.5% of treated patients; early recognition and steroid therapy are critical.
HLA-DRB1*03:01 confers a two- to fourfold increased risk of dermatomyositis and anti-synthetase syndrome; other HLA associations differ by subtype and ancestry.
Myositis occurs as an overlap syndrome in roughly 10–25% of cases of systemic lupus erythematosus, systemic sclerosis, and Sjögren syndrome, where it adds to the inflammatory burden.

UV radiation activates type I interferon pathways in skin and muscle; dermatomyositis patients frequently report that flares are triggered by sun exposure, and high UV latitudes show higher incidence.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 45–65 with dermatomyositis or polymyositis
Men over 50 with inclusion body myositis
Adults over 40 with new-onset myositis plus cancer risk factors (paraneoplastic work-up indicated)
Statin-treated patients, especially those on high-intensity regimens
Patients receiving immune checkpoint inhibitors for cancer
diagnosisOverview: Myositis is diagnosed through a convergence of clinical features, laboratory tests, electromyography, imaging, and muscle biopsy. The most informative initial test is serum creatine kinase (CK), which is elevated in most forms — often 10 to 50 times the upper limit of normal in polymyositis and IMNM — though it may be normal in dermatomyositis and is only mildly raised in IBM. A panel of myositis-specific and myositis-associated antibodies (including anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, anti-SRP, anti-HMGCR, anti-MDA5) is now a standard part of work-up because it guides prognosis and treatment and identifies high-risk subsets (anti-MDA5 is associated with rapidly progressive ILD). Electromyography shows irritable myopathy with fibrillations, positive sharp waves, and short-duration, low-amplitude motor unit potentials, helping distinguish myositis from a neurogenic cause of weakness. MRI of affected muscles, particularly the thighs, reveals oedema and inflammation before weakness is pronounced and guides where to biopsy. Muscle biopsy remains the gold standard for diagnosis and subtyping: it identifies the immune cell infiltrate pattern (endomysial in polymyositis, perimysial in dermatomyositis, rimmed vacuoles and filamentous inclusions in IBM), confirming the diagnosis and ruling out muscular dystrophy or toxic myopathy. Pulmonary function tests and high-resolution CT of the chest are performed when interstitial lung disease is suspected. Adults over 40 with new myositis need a structured malignancy screen (CT chest-abdomen-pelvis, tumour markers) given the paraneoplastic association.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum creatine kinase (CK) and muscle enzymes
diagnosticTests.purpose: CK reflects the extent of muscle fibre damage; very high levels (>10× normal) support active myositis and help monitor treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within hours. CK may be checked every few weeks to track improvement with therapy.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, anti-SRP, anti-HMGCR, anti-MDA5, and anti-TIF1γ antibodies each predict a distinct clinical subtype, prognosis, and treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample sent to a specialist immunology laboratory; results may take 1–2 weeks and significantly shape the management plan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the characteristic pattern of irritable myopathy and excludes neuropathic or neuromuscular junction causes of weakness.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Thin needle electrodes are inserted into several muscles; the procedure takes 30–60 minutes and may cause mild discomfort and temporary bruising.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies oedematous, inflamed muscles before weakness is severe; guides biopsy site selection and monitors treatment response without the risks of biopsy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard MRI scan of the target region, lasting 30–45 minutes; no radiation involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard test that confirms inflammatory myopathy, defines the subtype histologically, and excludes muscular dystrophy or metabolic myopathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small tissue sample is taken under local anaesthetic, usually from a moderately weak muscle identified by MRI; the procedure takes about 20–30 minutes, with mild soreness for a few days.

diagnosticTests.test: High-resolution CT chest and pulmonary function tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects interstitial lung disease, which complicates anti-synthetase syndrome and dermatomyositis and is the leading cause of myositis-related mortality.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief CT scan (no contrast usually required) and spirometry with diffusion capacity testing; results guide whether to escalate immunosuppression.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of inflammatory myositis centres on suppressing the abnormal immune response to arrest muscle damage and restore function. High-dose corticosteroids are the cornerstone: oral prednisolone at 1 mg/kg per day (up to 60–80 mg) is started promptly and tapered over 12–18 months once CK normalises and strength improves. Because long-term high-dose steroids produce significant side effects — osteoporosis, diabetes, infections, adrenal suppression — a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant is introduced within 4–8 weeks for most patients. Methotrexate and azathioprine are the most widely used first-line steroid-sparing agents, with mycophenolate mofetil preferred when interstitial lung disease is prominent. Patients with refractory disease, rapidly worsening ILD, or anti-MDA5 antibodies often receive intravenous methylprednisolone pulses, rituximab, or intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG). IVIG is particularly valued in refractory dermatomyositis, where a phase III trial (ProDERM) showed that IVIG 2 g/kg monthly produced clinically meaningful improvement versus placebo. Inclusion body myositis is largely resistant to immunosuppression; physiotherapy is the primary intervention. Physical and occupational therapy are integral to all forms of myositis, both to rehabilitate muscle strength and to preserve function in the joints. Treatment of the specific trigger (statin withdrawal, cancer therapy, antibiotics for infectious myositis) is essential in secondary forms. Goals are remission, steroid taper to the lowest effective dose, and prevention of relapse.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone/methylprednisolone)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy for most inflammatory myopathies; oral prednisolone at 1 mg/kg/day induces remission in the majority of patients with polymyositis and dermatomyositis within 3–6 months, with the dose tapered slowly over 12–18 months to avoid relapse.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial response (CK normalisation) in approximately 70–80% of polymyositis and dermatomyositis patients within 3 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate
medicalTreatments.description: The most widely used steroid-sparing agent; taken orally once weekly at 10–25 mg with folic acid supplementation; reduces steroid requirements and maintains remission; relatively contraindicated in significant lung disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Allows prednisolone dose reduction in approximately 60–70% of patients in observational series; reduces relapse risk compared with steroids alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative steroid-sparing agent used when methotrexate is unsuitable (interstitial lung disease, hepatic disease, or methotrexate intolerance); requires TPMT genotyping before starting to identify patients at risk of bone marrow suppression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains remission and facilitates steroid reduction in roughly 55–65% of patients in open-label series.

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred steroid-sparing agent when interstitial lung disease is present; inhibits purine synthesis in lymphocytes; also used in overlap myositis with systemic sclerosis or lupus.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilises or improves pulmonary function in approximately 50–70% of myositis-associated ILD cases in retrospective studies.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
medicalTreatments.description: 2 g/kg given over 2–5 days every 4 weeks; the ProDERM phase III trial in 2021 demonstrated significant improvement in dermatomyositis; also used in refractory polymyositis and myopathy with dysphagia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ProDERM trial showed 79% response rate versus 44% with placebo in adult dermatomyositis over 16 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab
medicalTreatments.description: An anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody that depletes B cells; used in refractory myositis, particularly in patients with anti-Jo-1, anti-Mi-2, or anti-MDA5 antibodies; given as two infusions 2 weeks apart.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: The RIM trial showed approximately 83% of refractory patients met a predetermined improvement threshold, with responses often delayed 3–6 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supervised physiotherapy and exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured aerobic and resistance exercise — historically deferred for fear of aggravating inflammation — has been shown in multiple RCTs to improve muscle strength and function in stable myositis without raising CK, and is now recommended throughout the disease course.

alternativeTreatments.name: Occupational therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Adaptive equipment (raised toilet seats, grab rails, jar openers) and compensatory techniques for daily activities preserve independence while strength is recovering.

alternativeTreatments.name: Speech and swallowing therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Dysphagia affects up to 30% of patients; swallowing exercises and dietary texture modification reduce aspiration risk, which is a leading cause of myositis-related morbidity.

whySeeSpecialist: Myositis requires rheumatological evaluation to confirm the diagnosis, identify the specific subtype, screen for malignancy and interstitial lung disease, and initiate immunosuppression safely. A rheumatologist can also interpret the myositis antibody panel, coordinate pulmonary and oncological input, and monitor for the serious side effects of long-term steroids and immunosuppressants. Neurological review is valuable when inclusion body myositis or a neurogenic cause of weakness is in question. Early specialist involvement reduces the risk of permanent muscle damage from delayed treatment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a rheumatologist with a subspecialty interest in myositis or inflammatory muscle disease, ideally in a centre that performs muscle biopsy and has an on-site myositis antibody panel. Access to a multidisciplinary team including a respiratory physician (for ILD surveillance), a neurologist, and an experienced physiotherapist experienced in inflammatory myopathy is important for complex cases. When dermatomyositis is suspected, an age-appropriate malignancy screen should be part of the initial evaluation.
preventionStrategies: There is no established way to prevent idiopathic inflammatory myositis, but modifiable triggers can be addressed.

Patients on statins who develop unexplained muscle pain or CK elevation should have statin therapy reviewed and an anti-HMGCR antibody test considered before attributing the symptoms to statin myalgia.
Minimise unprotected sun exposure in dermatomyositis, where UV radiation is a recognised flare trigger.
Monitor for interstitial lung disease with annual pulmonary function tests and prompt CT scanning when respiratory symptoms develop.
Screen adults over 40 with new-onset dermatomyositis for underlying malignancy at diagnosis and again after 3 years.
lifestyleModifications: Continue supervised exercise throughout the treatment course; physical deconditioning worsens functional outcomes independently of muscle inflammation.
Use sun protection (SPF 50+ sunscreen, protective clothing) in dermatomyositis, where UV is a disease trigger.
Ensure adequate protein intake (1.2–1.5 g/kg body weight daily) to support muscle repair during recovery.
Fall prevention is important during active weakness — remove trip hazards, install grab rails, and use a walking aid if unsteady.
Bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonate when indicated) is essential for all patients on prolonged corticosteroids.
Avoid starting or escalating statins during an active flare; discuss alternatives with the prescribing clinician.

recommended: Protein-rich foods (lean meat, fish, eggs, legumes, dairy) to support muscle repair
Calcium and vitamin D from dairy, fortified foods, and sunlight to protect bones during steroid therapy
Anti-inflammatory dietary patterns (Mediterranean diet) rich in oily fish, olive oil, and vegetables
Soft or pureed foods and thickened liquids if dysphagia is present
avoid: Excess sodium, which worsens corticosteroid-induced fluid retention and hypertension
Added sugars and refined carbohydrates, which exacerbate steroid-induced blood glucose elevation
Grapefruit and pomelo, which can interfere with some immunosuppressant metabolism
Very low-calorie diets, which impair muscle recovery and immune function

exerciseGuidelines: Contrary to historical practice, exercise does not worsen inflammatory myositis in the stable or maintenance phase. Randomised controlled trials demonstrate that both aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming at moderate intensity 3–5 times per week) and resistance training (2–3 sessions per week, progressing gradually) improve muscle strength, aerobic capacity, and quality of life without raising CK or triggering relapse. During acute active disease with very high CK and significant weakness, activities should be moderated and supervised physiotherapy preferred. A physiotherapist familiar with inflammatory myopathy should design the programme and advance it according to the individual's response.
dailyManagement: Take medications as prescribed; do not reduce corticosteroids without medical guidance, as abrupt reduction triggers relapse in a majority of patients.
Monitor for signs of steroid side effects: blood glucose elevation, blood pressure changes, mood changes, weight gain, and new bruising.
Attend regular blood tests to monitor CK, liver function (especially with methotrexate), and full blood count.
Report new or worsening shortness of breath promptly — it may signal progressive ILD requiring urgent escalation.
Keep a symptom diary noting weakness levels, activity tolerance, and skin changes to detect early flares.
Maintain an updated list of all immunosuppressive medications when seeking other medical care, as infection risk is elevated.
prognosis: Prognosis in myositis is heterogeneous and depends strongly on the subtype, the presence of interstitial lung disease, associated malignancy, and response to initial treatment. Dermatomyositis and polymyositis that respond to first-line steroids carry a 5-year survival above 90% in contemporary cohorts; approximately 50–60% of patients achieve sustained remission on maintenance therapy, while 30–40% have a relapsing-remitting course requiring long-term immunosuppression. Interstitial lung disease, particularly the rapidly progressive form associated with anti-MDA5 antibodies, is the principal cause of death and can reduce 1-year survival to 50% or below in the most severe cases. Inclusion body myositis has a different trajectory: strength declines slowly but steadily over years, and most patients require a walking aid within 10–15 years of diagnosis, with immunosuppression showing no consistent benefit. Malignancy-associated myositis largely depends on cancer outcome. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment remain the most modifiable determinants of a good outcome.
recoveryTimeline: After starting corticosteroids, CK usually begins to fall within 2–4 weeks, which precedes recovery of strength by several weeks to months. Most patients notice meaningful functional improvement — easier stair climbing, better overhead reach — within 3–6 months of starting treatment. Full restoration of pre-illness strength may take 12–24 months and is not achieved in all patients. Steroid tapering typically begins once CK has been normal and symptoms stable for 3–4 months, with the taper extending over at least 12–18 months to prevent relapse. Some patients require low-dose maintenance immunosuppression indefinitely. Inclusion body myositis follows a different timeline: function declines gradually over years and rehabilitation rather than remission is the goal.
complications: Interstitial lung disease — occurs in up to 40% of patients, especially those with anti-synthetase or anti-MDA5 antibodies, and is the leading cause of mortality.
Aspiration pneumonia — from dysphagia and weakened pharyngeal muscles; can be life-threatening and requires swallowing assessment.
Steroid and immunosuppressant side effects — including osteoporotic fractures, diabetes, serious infections, avascular necrosis of the hip, and gastrointestinal complications.
Malignancy — adults over 40 with dermatomyositis have a 3 to 7 times elevated cancer risk in the first 3 years after diagnosis; screening is mandatory.
Cardiac involvement — myocarditis, conduction defects, and heart failure occur in 10–20% of cases and may be subclinical.
Permanent muscle weakness and disability — particularly in IBM and in cases where diagnosis or treatment is delayed beyond several months.

supportResources.name: Myositis Association
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy organisation offering education, support groups, and a specialist physician directory for people with inflammatory myopathies.

supportResources.name: NIH — Myositis Information Page
supportResources.description: Overview of myositis types, causes, and treatments from the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.

supportResources.name: Versus Arthritis — Myositis
supportResources.description: UK patient charity resources covering polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and inclusion body myositis, including practical daily living advice.

sources.title: Lundberg IE et al. 2017 European League Against Rheumatism/American College of Rheumatology classification criteria for adult and juvenile idiopathic inflammatory myopathies. Ann Rheum Dis 2017.
Oddis CV et al. Rituximab in the treatment of refractory adult and juvenile dermatomyositis and adult polymyositis: a randomized, placebo-phase trial (RIM trial). Arthritis Rheum 2013.
Aggarwal R et al. Prospective, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled phase III study evaluating efficacy and safety of IVIG-SQ in dermatomyositis (ProDERM). Lancet 2022.
Allenbach Y et al. 224th ENMC International Workshop: Clinico-sero-pathological classification of immune-mediated necrotising myopathies. Neuromuscul Disord 2018.
British Society for Rheumatology — BSR and BHPR guidelines for the management of polymyositis and dermatomyositis (2017 update).
NIH National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke — Myositis Information Page.
Dalakas MC. Inflammatory muscle diseases. New England Journal of Medicine 2015.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread musculoskeletal pain and fatigue with normal CK, normal muscle biopsy, and no true weakness; myositis produces objectively measurable weakness and elevated muscle enzymes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyalgia rheumatica
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyalgia rheumatica causes shoulder and pelvic girdle pain and stiffness in those over 50 but without significant weakness or CK elevation; it responds rapidly to low-dose prednisolone, unlike myositis which requires higher doses.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rhabdomyolysis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rhabdomyolysis is an acute catastrophic muscle breakdown with CK often >10,000 U/L, myoglobinuria, and risk of acute kidney injury; it is usually caused by a single trigger (exercise, trauma, drug) and resolves with hydration rather than immunosuppression.

confusedWithConditions.name: Muscular dystrophy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Muscular dystrophies are inherited disorders with onset typically in childhood and a slowly progressive course; they lack the inflammatory infiltrate on biopsy that defines myositis and do not respond to steroids (except for Duchenne, where steroids slow progression).

relatedConditions.name: Dermatomyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common named subtype of inflammatory myositis in adults, distinguishable by characteristic skin findings and an elevated malignancy risk.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: A named inflammatory myopathy subtype causing pure proximal weakness without skin involvement, sharing the same treatment approach as dermatomyositis.

relatedConditions.name: Rhabdomyolysis
relatedConditions.relevance: An acute form of severe muscle destruction that can complicate untreated or fulminant inflammatory myositis, or arise independently from drugs, extreme exertion, or trauma.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently appears in the differential diagnosis of myositis and may coexist, particularly contributing to pain and fatigue after myositis is controlled.

faqs.question: What is myositis and how is it different from ordinary muscle soreness?
faqs.answer: Myositis is autoimmune or infectious inflammation of skeletal muscle that causes progressive weakness, elevated blood enzymes, and — on biopsy — immune cell infiltration of muscle fibres. Ordinary muscle soreness after exercise is a temporary, self-resolving microtrauma without weakness, enzyme elevation, or inflammation on biopsy.

faqs.question: What are the main types of inflammatory myositis?
faqs.answer: The main types are polymyositis, dermatomyositis, inclusion body myositis, immune-mediated necrotising myopathy, and anti-synthetase syndrome. Each differs in the immune mechanism, the muscles most affected, associated features such as skin rash or lung disease, and the response to treatment.

faqs.question: What does a very high creatine kinase mean in myositis?
faqs.answer: Creatine kinase leaks from damaged muscle fibres into the blood. Levels more than 10 times the upper limit of normal strongly support active muscle inflammation and guide the urgency of treatment. CK falling toward normal during therapy indicates the inflammation is being controlled. A very high CK with urine discolouration may signal rhabdomyolysis, which needs urgent hospital care.

faqs.question: Can myositis be caused by a statin?
faqs.answer: Yes. Statins can cause a spectrum of muscle problems, from simple myalgia to immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (IMNM), which persists after stopping the statin and requires immunosuppression. Anti-HMGCR antibodies confirm statin-triggered IMNM. Anyone on a statin who develops unexplained muscle weakness or a CK more than 10 times normal should stop the statin and seek specialist assessment.

faqs.question: Is myositis associated with cancer?
faqs.answer: Adults over 40 with dermatomyositis have a 3 to 7 times higher risk of cancer in the 3 years after diagnosis, most commonly ovarian, lung, colorectal, or nasopharyngeal. A structured malignancy screen — CT chest-abdomen-pelvis and relevant tumour markers — is a standard part of the initial work-up and is usually repeated after 3 years. Treating the cancer often improves the myositis.

faqs.question: How long does myositis treatment last?
faqs.answer: Most patients need at least 2–3 years of treatment. Corticosteroids are started at high dose and tapered slowly over 12–18 months once CK normalises and strength improves. A steroid-sparing immunosuppressant is usually added and continued for years to maintain remission and allow a lower steroid dose. Some patients require indefinite low-dose maintenance therapy.

faqs.question: Can I exercise if I have myositis?
faqs.answer: Yes, and it is recommended. Multiple clinical trials show that supervised aerobic and resistance exercise improves muscle strength and function in stable myositis without worsening inflammation or raising CK. During acute active disease, exercise is moderated, but rehabilitation exercise should be part of management from the outset under physiotherapy guidance.

faqs.question: What is inclusion body myositis and why is it different?
faqs.answer: Inclusion body myositis (IBM) is the most common inflammatory myopathy in people over 50. Unlike polymyositis and dermatomyositis, IBM affects both proximal and distal muscles — characteristically the finger flexors and quadriceps — and is largely resistant to immunosuppression. Treatment focuses on physiotherapy and fall prevention rather than on achieving remission.

faqs.question: What is the risk of lung disease with myositis?
faqs.answer: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) occurs in up to 40% of patients with inflammatory myositis, particularly those with anti-synthetase syndrome or anti-MDA5 antibodies. Rapidly progressive ILD is the leading cause of death in myositis and can deteriorate within weeks. Pulmonary function tests and CT of the chest should be performed at diagnosis and repeated if breathlessness develops.

faqs.question: Does myositis cause skin problems?
faqs.answer: Dermatomyositis specifically produces characteristic skin findings: a violet-tinged heliotrope rash around the eyes, Gottron papules over the knuckles, a shawl-sign rash across the shoulders, and, in anti-synthetase syndrome, cracked skin on the fingertips called mechanic's hands. Polymyositis and inclusion body myositis do not cause skin rashes.

faqs.question: How is myositis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical features (proximal weakness pattern), serum creatine kinase, myositis-specific antibody testing, electromyography, MRI of affected muscles, and muscle biopsy. Biopsy is the gold standard. Pulmonary function tests and CT of the chest are added when lung involvement is suspected.

faqs.question: What medications are used to treat myositis?
faqs.answer: High-dose prednisolone is the cornerstone treatment. A steroid-sparing immunosuppressant — methotrexate, azathioprine, or mycophenolate — is added within 4–8 weeks. Refractory cases may need intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) or rituximab. Inclusion body myositis does not respond reliably to these agents.

faqs.question: Can myositis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse is common: roughly 30–40% of patients have a relapsing-remitting course. Relapses often occur when steroids are tapered too quickly or when the immunosuppressant dose is reduced. A rising CK, even before new weakness develops, can signal a relapse. Regular blood tests and prompt escalation of therapy help prevent significant muscle damage during relapse.

faqs.question: Is myositis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Idiopathic inflammatory myositis is not directly inherited, but certain HLA genetic variants increase susceptibility. A family history of autoimmune disease raises the background risk slightly. Inclusion body myositis has a sporadic and a hereditary form (hereditary IBM, which is distinct from the idiopathic form and not inflammatory).

faqs.question: What are the warning signs that myositis is getting worse?
faqs.answer: Rising CK on blood tests, increasing difficulty with stairs or overhead tasks, new shortness of breath, worsening swallowing difficulty, and new skin rash activity all suggest worsening disease. Any of these should prompt early contact with the treating specialist rather than waiting for the next scheduled appointment.

faqs.question: Can myositis affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Cardiac involvement — including myocarditis, conduction abnormalities, and heart failure — occurs in 10–20% of myositis patients and may be clinically silent. An ECG and cardiac enzymes are checked at diagnosis, and referral to a cardiologist is appropriate if symptoms such as palpitations, chest pain, or unexplained breathlessness develop.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis of myositis?
faqs.answer: For dermatomyositis and polymyositis that respond to first-line treatment, 5-year survival exceeds 90% in modern series. About half of patients achieve sustained remission. Prognosis worsens significantly with interstitial lung disease, associated malignancy, or delayed treatment. Inclusion body myositis progresses steadily, often requiring walking aids within 10–15 years.

faqs.question: When should I seek emergency care for myositis?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if you develop sudden severe respiratory distress suggesting respiratory muscle failure, inability to swallow saliva safely, profound new weakness over hours to days, or fever with very swollen and painful muscle pointing to bacterial infection. These are rare but serious complications that need immediate hospital assessment.

faqs.question: Are there support groups for people with myositis?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Myositis Association (myositis.org) in the United States offers peer support groups, patient education, and a specialist physician directory. Versus Arthritis in the UK provides information and local support. Connecting with others who have the condition helps many patients manage the practical and emotional challenges of a long-term inflammatory disease.

faqs.question: What dietary changes help myositis?
faqs.answer: A protein-rich diet supports muscle repair; aim for 1.2–1.5 g of protein per kilogram of body weight daily. Calcium and vitamin D are important for bone protection during steroid therapy. A Mediterranean-style anti-inflammatory dietary pattern may complement medical treatment. Restrict sodium and refined carbohydrates to mitigate steroid-induced fluid retention and blood glucose rise.

metaDescription: Myositis is autoimmune muscle inflammation causing progressive weakness and elevated CK. Learn subtypes, diagnosis, and treatment options. Doctor-reviewed.
